benchmark_name: Temporal Anomaly Detection in Visual Artifacts
description: This benchmark tests whether vision language models correctly understand the temporal relationships between artifacts and can detect anachronisms.

questions.question_text: Which artifact belongs to a different time period than the others?

metadata.title: Architectural Stone Slab
metadata.brief_description:
Architectural stone slab showing standing figures of a divine 
couple in the central panel made of two pillar. In the recessed 
panels, two standing figures each are carved.  Both of they hold 
an object in their right hands.  Towards right there is one more 
recessed panel which contain standing figure of a lady.

metadata.title: Ma'bar Coin
metadata.brief_description:
Madurai Sultanate also known as Ma’bar
Sultanate was a short lived dynasty based in the city of Madurai in present day
Tamil Nadu. The Sultanate was declared independent by Jalal-ud-Din Ahsan Khan
from the Delhi Sultanate. The time span of Madurai Sultanate was very brief
lasting for a period of forty three years ruled by eight different rulers.

metadata.title: Punchmarked Coin
metadata.brief_description: The term ‘Punch mark coins’ was first coined by James Prinsep in the year 1835 C.E. Vast majority of scholars believe that the earliest Punch Marked coins were issued by silver smith or bankers, but later on, the state tookover the responsibility for issuing coins and it its prerogative.
metadata.detailed_description:
South Asia has been the cradle of human civilisation since time immemorial. The river basins on the North and North western parts on India have been occupied by humans since 5000 B.C.E. These people engaged in trade and commerce with people from several other parts of the world including other civilisations. Trading was initially done by using Barter system but, with the advent of more complex economic transactions the coins became the medium of commercial exchange. 
According to archaeological evidences the earliest coins in India date back to 6th-5th Century B.C.E. These are the famous Punch marked coins mainly made of silver and copper. Mostly rectangular in shape, occasionally square or round with symbols. These symbols were impressed by separate punches at various points on the surface of the coin.
They often overlap and on certain coins where the symbols are numerous, this overlapping results in a confused jumble. Rarely are obverse and reverse both the surfaces of the coin equally crowded with symbols. These symbols are regarded as the official guarantee of the validity of the coin and are considered, variously, as of religious or mythological in nature, or as official marks or symbols of the ruling dynasty or king, or as marks of bankers.

metadata.title: Earth Goddess
metadata.brief_description: The Earth Goddess carved on a gold leaf was found with a small deposit of human bones inside a funeral mound at Lauriya in the district of Champaran. The excavations were conducted by N.G.Majumdar in March,1905, and the identification of the female figure as Prithivi, the Earth Goddess, has been made with the help of a verse in the Rigveda.

square base decorated with highly ornamental Ghata-pallava motif 
and an octagonal shaft the faces of which are covered with 
floriated bands. The square abacus is decorated with Kirltimukhas 
emitting floriated scrolls. The capital rests on a triple molding. 
On the top there is an ardhapadma motif with over flowing foliage 
at the corners.

metadata.title: Album No 14 (A Sketch)
metadata.museum_name: National Gallery of Modern Art, New Delhi
metadata.brief_description: Nandalal, in his sketches, experimented with the representation of lines to attain texture and add vigour and life to the form of his figures. This sketch portrays a human figure in a seated position on the floor from the back, rendered in quick and subtle contouring lines. The fluid handling of the line and the dark ink strokes suggest the modelling of the body, sparing much detail.
metadata.detailed_description: Nandalal Bose had a strong affinity for sketching, his subjects being the surrounding environs, people in their everyday life or anything that he found fascinating. This album mostly consists of portraits and figurative sketches of people. Their myriad poses served as templates for his sketching routines. Nandalal's sketches vastly vary in detailing, some drawn quickly with just a few lines, others executed with more elaborate characteristics from an analytical perspective. In his book 'On Art', he writes, "An oriental artist need not start with painstaking labour of acquiring anatomical knowledge. For him an understanding of the life-movement shall suffice as a beginning. The principal life-movement in his drawing should never be lost or obscured in a temptation to lay stress on details of form and features."

metadata.title: Ma'bar Coin
metadata.brief_description:
Madurai Sultanate also known as Ma’bar
Sultanate was a short lived dynasty based in the city of Madurai in present day
Tamil Nadu. The Sultanate was declared independent by Jalal-ud-Din Ahsan Khan
from the Delhi Sultanate. The time span of Madurai Sultanate was very brief
lasting for a period of forty three years ruled by eight different rulers.

metadata.title: Punchmarked Coin
metadata.brief_description: The term ‘Punch mark coins’ was first coined by James Prinsep in the year 1835 C.E. Vast majority of scholars believe that the earliest Punch Marked coins were issued by silver smith or bankers, but later on, the state tookover the responsibility for issuing coins and it its prerogative.
metadata.detailed_description: South Asia has been the cradle of human civilisation since time immemorial. The river basins on the North and North western parts of India have been occupied by humans since 5000 B.C.E. These people were engaged in trade and commerce with people from several other parts of the world including other civilisations. Trading was initially done by using Barter system but, with the advent of more complex economic transactions the coins became the medium of commercial exchange. According to archaeological evidences the earliest coins in India date back to 6th-5th Century B.C.E. These are the famous Punch marked coins mainly made of silver and copper. Mostly rectangular in shape, occasionally square or round with symbols. These symbols were impressed by separate punches at various points on the surface of the coin. They often overlap and on certain coins where the symbols are numerous, this overlapping results in a confused jumble. Rarely are obverse and reverse both the surfaces of the coin equally crowded with symbols. These symbols are regarded as the official guarantee of the validity of the coin and are considered, variously, as of religious or mythological in nature, or as official marks or symbols of the ruling dynasty or king, or as marks of bankers.

metadata.title: Image of Narasimha
metadata.brief_description:
Image of Narasimha assassinating the demon
Hirnyakashapu. The hybrid god has flowing hairs reaching up to shoulders and
secured by a crown. He wears hara, armlets and bracelets. The god is standing
on his right leg while his left foot is placed on a demon crawling beneath the
left leg. The body of the demon Hirnyakahapu is thrown on the thighs of left
leg and the god is engaged in tearing it with his two hands. His other two
hands are in abhaya-mudra and hold shankha. The demon holds a dagger and a
shield. The back slab behind shows a horizontal beam upon which lotus petals
are carved.

metadata.title: A Sketch from Album No 80
metadata.museum_name: National Gallery of Modern Art, New Delhi
metadata.main_material: Pen and ink and pencil on postcard
metadata.brief_description:
Nandalal had a strong
affinity for sketching, his subjects being the surrounding environs, people in
their everyday life or anything that he found fascinating. In this sketch, he
has depicted a man carrying a heavy bulk of leafs on his shoulder, executed in
fluid, bold strokes of ink. The artist has carefully rendered the muscle
tension of the figure in fine lines, that brings out the corporeality in the form.
metadata.detailed_description: The National Gallery of Modern Art, New Delhi acquired the largest collection of 6744 artworks of Nandalal Bose from his family some of which were exhibited in the Centenary exhibition organised by the museum in the year 1983. A major portion of the museum's collection includes monochromatic pencil sketches, pen and ink drawings on postcard and paper, watercolour paintings and sketches apart from the more detailed and elaborate tempera and wash paintings, Haripura posters and prints from the artist's extensive oeuvre. This album of 21 works mostly consists of a scene from the Poush fair that took place in Santiniketan once in a year during the month of Poush, which is marked as a harvesting season.

metadata.title: Punchmarked Coin
metadata.brief_description: The term ‘Punch mark coins’ was first coined by James Prinsep in the year 1835 C.E. Vast majority of scholars believe that the earliest Punch Marked coins were issued by silver smith or bankers, but later on, the state tookover the responsibility for issuing coins and it its prerogative.
metadata.detailed_description: South Asia has been the cradle of human civilisation since time immemorial. The river basins on the North and North western parts of India have been occupied by humans since 5000 B.C.E. These people were engaged in trade and commerce with people from several other parts of the world including other civilisations. Trading was initially done by using Barter system but, with the advent of more complex economic transactions the coins became the medium of commercial exchange. According to archaeological evidences the earliest coins in India date back to 6th-5th Century B.C.E. These are the famous Punch marked coins mainly made of silver and copper. Mostly rectangular in shape, occasionally square or round with symbols. These symbols were impressed by separate punches at various points on the surface of the coin. They often overlap and on certain coins where the symbols are numerous, this overlapping results in a confused jumble. Rarely are obverse and reverse both the surfaces of the coin equally crowded with symbols. These symbols are regarded as the official guarantee of the validity of the coin and are considered, variously, as of religious or mythological in nature, or as official marks or symbols of the ruling dynasty or king, or as marks of bankers.

metadata.title: Buddha Bust  Stele Fragment
metadata.brief_description: The Buddha bust is shown on stele fragment.
metadata.detailed_description: The sculpture is broken so that only the head and part of the right shoulder remain.The Buddha, nimbate, wears a diaphanous samghati with raised ridges depicting folds as are found on Buddha images of the Gupta period from Mathura. Folds of this type are not generally seen on images from this area. The face has plump cheeks, a bow-shaped mouth, and raised eye-brows. The folds of flesh on the neck are emphasized. A flying Deva bearing a garland can be seen above the figure. Part of the cross-bar of a throne back can be seen in low relief behind the right shoulder. Stylistically this head is reminiscentury of Sarnath images made after the fifth century CE.

metadata.title: Hanuman carrying Mount Dron with Sanjivini herb
metadata.brief_description:
Hanuman who abounds in immense knowledge, unlimited virtue, 
incomparable might, wind's pace and earth's firmness is not only 
the widest known god but in the entire Hindu pantheon also the 
most widely worshipped divinity. Hanuman, the emissary of Lord 
Rama, is known by many names such as Mahavira, Mahabali, 
Sankatmochan, Maruti, Ramaduta and Bajrangbali. Uttering his name, 
considered to have mystic powers of a mantra, dispels evil and 
commemorating it brings success in examinations, politics, 
business, sports, family-life and other worldly matters. He is 
believed to undo adverse influence of unfavorable stars. 
This highly powerful image of the 'Ramabhakta', Hanuman carrying 
Mount Dron is a beautiful depiction of the valorous act by the 
Monkey god. In this painting he is depicted carrying Mount Dron, 
one of the most wondrous acts that he accomplished for his lord 
Rama. Hit by Meghanatha's celestial weapon, Lakshmana, the younger 
brother of Rama, was wounded and had fallen unconscious. The herb 
he could be revived with was available only on Mount Dron in the 
far north. Hanuman went to fetch the herb but was unable to 
identify it. He hence uprooted the entire mountain and brought it 
to Rama's camp in Lanka. 
In most of the Hanuman paintings and sculptures his tail has been 
usually projected with prominence. In the legends associated with 
Hanuman, he is said to have performed most of his valorous deeds 
including the burning of Lanka with his tail. Hanuman could expand 
it to any length, make it tough and put into it any volume of 
weight. Here in the painting extra toughness and length given to 
Hanuman's tail seems to indicate that with its weight and length 
Hanuman has sought to balance the mountain's weight carried 
opposite to it while flying.  The jewellery - golden girdle, 
anklets, armbands and large earrings are an adding feature to the 
majestic personality of Hanuman. The contrast of green and orange 
colours is typical of Raghogarh painting from Central India.

metadata.title: Ma'bar Coin

Madurai Sultanate also known as Ma’bar
Sultanate was a short lived dynasty based in the city of Madurai in present day
Tamil Nadu. The Sultanate was declared independent by Jalal-ud-Din Ahsan Khan
from the Delhi Sultanate. The time span of Madurai Sultanate was very brief
lasting for a period of forty three years ruled by eight different rulers.

metadata.title: Image of Narasimha
metadata.brief_description:
Image of Narasimha assassinating the demon
Hirnyakashapu. The hybrid god has flowing hairs reaching up to shoulders and
secured by a crown. He wears hara, armlets and bracelets. The god is standing
on his right leg while his left foot is placed on a demon crawling beneath the
left leg. The body of the demon Hirnyakahapu is thrown on the thighs of left
leg and the god is engaged in tearing it with his two hands. His other two
hands are in abhaya-mudra and hold shankha. The demon holds a dagger and a
shield. The back slab behind shows a horizontal beam upon which lotus petals
are carved.

metadata.title: Royal Saint
metadata.brief_description:

saint having flowing moustache and well-trimmed bearded, standing on a
double-lotus pedestal, which is mounted on a square block with a flat base
having lotus petal decoration all around. The weight is taken on right leg. The
left leg having flexed and slightly advanced. Both the hands are joined against
the breast in Anjali-mudra. Other details include a fillet with tassels, two
necklaces, a beaded hara, patra-kundalas , armlets, bracelets etc.

metadata.title: Punchmarked Coin
metadata.brief_description: The term ‘Punch mark coins’ was first coined by James Prinsep in the year 1835 C.E. Vast majority of scholars believe that the earliest Punch Marked coins were issued by silver smith or bankers, but later on, the state tookover the responsibility for issuing coins and it its prerogative.
metadata.detailed_description: South Asia has been the cradle of human civilisation since time immemorial. The river basins on the North and North western parts of India have been occupied by humans since 5000 B.C.E. These people were engaged in trade and commerce with people from several other parts of the world including other civilisations. Trading was initially done by using Barter system but, with the advent of more complex economic transactions the coins became the medium of commercial exchange. According to archaeological evidences the earliest coins in India date back to 6th-5th Century B.C.E. These are the famous Punch marked coins mainly made of silver and copper. Mostly rectangular in shape, occasionally square or round with symbols. These symbols were impressed by separate punches at various points on the surface of the coin. They often overlap and on certain coins where the symbols are numerous, this overlapping results in a confused jumble. Rarely are obverse and reverse both the surfaces of the coin equally crowded with symbols. These symbols are regarded as the official guarantee of the validity of the coin and are considered, variously, as of religious or mythological in nature, or as official marks or symbols of the ruling dynasty or king, or as marks of bankers.

metadata.title: Punchmarked Coin
metadata.brief_description:
The
term ‘Punch mark coins’ was first coined by James Prinsep in the year 1835 C.E.
Vast majority of scholars believe that the earliest Punch Marked coins were
issued by silver smith or bankers, but later on, the state took over the
responsibility for issuing coins and it its prerogative.
metadata.detailed_description:
South Asia has been the cradle of human civilisation since time immemorial. The river basins on the North and North western parts on India have been occupied by humans since 5000 B.C.E. These people engaged in trade and commerce with people from several other parts of the world including other civilisations. Trading was initially done by using Barter system but, with the advent of more complex economic transactions the coins became the medium of commercial exchange. 
According to archaeological evidences the earliest coins in India date back to 6th-5th Century B.C.E. These are the famous Punch marked coins mainly made of silver and copper. Mostly rectangular in shape, occasionally square or round with symbols. These symbols were impressed by separate punches at various points on the surface of the coin.
They often overlap and on certain coins where the symbols are numerous, this overlapping results in a confused jumble. Rarely are obverse and reverse both the surfaces of the coin equally crowded with symbols. These symbols are regarded as the official guarantee of the validity of the coin and are considered, variously, as of religious or mythological in nature, or as official marks or symbols of the ruling dynasty or king, or as marks of bankers.

questions.explanation: This artifact is from the Classical Period (200 BCE - 650 AD). The appropriate techniques were available during this period, while the other techniques were developed later.

metadata.title: Pillar
metadata.brief_description:
Dwarfish square pillar, badly damaged with its two sides.  It has a 
square base decorated with highly ornamental Ghata-pallava motif 
and an octagonal shaft the faces of which are covered with 
floriated bands. The square abacus is decorated with Kirltimukhas 
emitting floriated scrolls. The capital rests on a triple molding. 
On the top there is an ardhapadma motif with over flowing foliage 
at the corners.

metadata.title: Punchmarked Coin
metadata.brief_description: The term ‘Punch mark coins’ was first coined by James Prinsep in the year 1835 C.E. Vast majority of scholars believe that the earliest Punch Marked coins were issued by silver smith or bankers, but later on, the state tookover the responsibility for issuing coins and it its prerogative.
metadata.detailed_description:
South Asia has been the cradle of human civilisation since time immemorial. The river basins on the North and North western parts on India have been occupied by humans since 5000 B.C.E. These people engaged in trade and commerce with people from several other parts of the world including other civilisations. Trading was initially done by using Barter system but, with the advent of more complex economic transactions the coins became the medium of commercial exchange. 
According to archaeological evidences the earliest coins in India date back to 6th-5th Century B.C.E. These are the famous Punch marked coins mainly made of silver and copper. Mostly rectangular in shape, occasionally square or round with symbols. These symbols were impressed by separate punches at various points on the surface of the coin.
They often overlap and on certain coins where the symbols are numerous, this overlapping results in a confused jumble. Rarely are obverse and reverse both the surfaces of the coin equally crowded with symbols. These symbols are regarded as the official guarantee of the validity of the coin and are considered, variously, as of religious or mythological in nature, or as official marks or symbols of the ruling dynasty or king, or as marks of bankers.

questions.explanation: This artifact is from the Iron Age (1800 - 200 BCE). The appropriate techniques were available during this period, while the other techniques were developed later.

questions.question_text: Which manufacturing techniques would have been available when this Terracotta was created?

metadata.title: Hanuman carrying Mount Dron with Sanjivini herb
metadata.brief_description:
Hanuman who abounds in immense knowledge, unlimited virtue, 
incomparable might, wind's pace and earth's firmness is not only 
the widest known god but in the entire Hindu pantheon also the 
most widely worshipped divinity. Hanuman, the emissary of Lord 
Rama, is known by many names such as Mahavira, Mahabali, 
Sankatmochan, Maruti, Ramaduta and Bajrangbali. Uttering his name, 
considered to have mystic powers of a mantra, dispels evil and 
commemorating it brings success in examinations, politics, 
business, sports, family-life and other worldly matters. He is 
believed to undo adverse influence of unfavorable stars. 
This highly powerful image of the 'Ramabhakta', Hanuman carrying 
Mount Dron is a beautiful depiction of the valorous act by the 
Monkey god. In this painting he is depicted carrying Mount Dron, 
one of the most wondrous acts that he accomplished for his lord 
Rama. Hit by Meghanatha's celestial weapon, Lakshmana, the younger 
brother of Rama, was wounded and had fallen unconscious. The herb 
he could be revived with was available only on Mount Dron in the 
far north. Hanuman went to fetch the herb but was unable to 
identify it. He hence uprooted the entire mountain and brought it 
to Rama's camp in Lanka. 
In most of the Hanuman paintings and sculptures his tail has been 
usually projected with prominence. In the legends associated with 
Hanuman, he is said to have performed most of his valorous deeds 
including the burning of Lanka with his tail. Hanuman could expand 
it to any length, make it tough and put into it any volume of 
weight. Here in the painting extra toughness and length given to 
Hanuman's tail seems to indicate that with its weight and length 
Hanuman has sought to balance the mountain's weight carried 
opposite to it while flying.  The jewellery - golden girdle, 
anklets, armbands and large earrings are an adding feature to the 
majestic personality of Hanuman. The contrast of green and orange 
colours is typical of Raghogarh painting from Central India.

South Asia has been the cradle of human civilisation since time immemorial. The river basins on the North and North western parts on India have been occupied by humans since 5000 B.C.E. These people engaged in trade and commerce with people from several other parts of the world including other civilisations. Trading was initially done by using Barter system but, with the advent of more complex economic transactions the coins became the medium of commercial exchange. 
According to archaeological evidences the earliest coins in India date back to 6th-5th Century B.C.E. These are the famous Punch marked coins mainly made of silver and copper. Mostly rectangular in shape, occasionally square or round with symbols. These symbols were impressed by separate punches at various points on the surface of the coin.
They often overlap and on certain coins where the symbols are numerous, this overlapping results in a confused jumble. Rarely are obverse and reverse both the surfaces of the coin equally crowded with symbols. These symbols are regarded as the official guarantee of the validity of the coin and are considered, variously, as of religious or mythological in nature, or as official marks or symbols of the ruling dynasty or king, or as marks of bankers.

secured by a crown. He wears hara, armlets and bracelets. The god is standing
on his right leg while his left foot is placed on a demon crawling beneath the

leg and the god is engaged in tearing it with his two hands. His other two
hands are in abhaya-mudra and hold shankha. The demon holds a dagger and a
shield. The back slab behind shows a horizontal beam upon which lotus petals
are carved.

metadata.title: A Sketch from Album No 80
metadata.museum_name: National Gallery of Modern Art, New Delhi
metadata.main_material: Pen and ink and pencil on postcard
metadata.brief_description:
Nandalal had a strong
affinity for sketching, his subjects being the surrounding environs, people in
their everyday life or anything that he found fascinating. In this sketch, he
has depicted a man carrying a heavy bulk of leafs on his shoulder, executed in
fluid, bold strokes of ink. The artist has carefully rendered the muscle
tension of the figure in fine lines, that brings out the corporeality in the form.
metadata.detailed_description: The National Gallery of Modern Art, New Delhi acquired the largest collection of 6744 artworks of Nandalal Bose from his family some of which were exhibited in the Centenary exhibition organised by the museum in the year 1983. A major portion of the museum's collection includes monochromatic pencil sketches, pen and ink drawings on postcard and paper, watercolour paintings and sketches apart from the more detailed and elaborate tempera and wash paintings, Haripura posters and prints from the artist's extensive oeuvre. This album of 21 works mostly consists of a scene from the Poush fair that took place in Santiniketan once in a year during the month of Poush, which is marked as a harvesting season.

metadata.title: Punchmarked Coin
metadata.brief_description:
The
term ‘Punch mark coins’ was first coined by James Prinsep in the year 1835 C.E.
Vast majority of scholars believe that the earliest Punch Marked coins were
issued by silver smith or bankers, but later on, the state took over the
responsibility for issuing coins and it its prerogative.
metadata.detailed_description:
South Asia has been the cradle of human civilisation since time immemorial. The river basins on the North and North western parts on India have been occupied by humans since 5000 B.C.E. These people engaged in trade and commerce with people from several other parts of the world including other civilisations. Trading was initially done by using Barter system but, with the advent of more complex economic transactions the coins became the medium of commercial exchange. 
According to archaeological evidences the earliest coins in India date back to 6th-5th Century B.C.E. These are the famous Punch marked coins mainly made of silver and copper. Mostly rectangular in shape, occasionally square or round with symbols. These symbols were impressed by separate punches at various points on the surface of the coin.
They often overlap and on certain coins where the symbols are numerous, this overlapping results in a confused jumble. Rarely are obverse and reverse both the surfaces of the coin equally crowded with symbols. These symbols are regarded as the official guarantee of the validity of the coin and are considered, variously, as of religious or mythological in nature, or as official marks or symbols of the ruling dynasty or king, or as marks of bankers.

metadata.title: Hanuman carrying Mount Dron with Sanjivini herb
metadata.brief_description:
Hanuman who abounds in immense knowledge, unlimited virtue, 
incomparable might, wind's pace and earth's firmness is not only 
the widest known god but in the entire Hindu pantheon also the 
most widely worshipped divinity. Hanuman, the emissary of Lord 
Rama, is known by many names such as Mahavira, Mahabali, 
Sankatmochan, Maruti, Ramaduta and Bajrangbali. Uttering his name, 
considered to have mystic powers of a mantra, dispels evil and 
commemorating it brings success in examinations, politics, 
business, sports, family-life and other worldly matters. He is 
believed to undo adverse influence of unfavorable stars. 
This highly powerful image of the 'Ramabhakta', Hanuman carrying 
Mount Dron is a beautiful depiction of the valorous act by the 
Monkey god. In this painting he is depicted carrying Mount Dron, 
one of the most wondrous acts that he accomplished for his lord 
Rama. Hit by Meghanatha's celestial weapon, Lakshmana, the younger 
brother of Rama, was wounded and had fallen unconscious. The herb 
he could be revived with was available only on Mount Dron in the 
far north. Hanuman went to fetch the herb but was unable to 
identify it. He hence uprooted the entire mountain and brought it 
to Rama's camp in Lanka. 
In most of the Hanuman paintings and sculptures his tail has been 
usually projected with prominence. In the legends associated with 
Hanuman, he is said to have performed most of his valorous deeds 
including the burning of Lanka with his tail. Hanuman could expand 
it to any length, make it tough and put into it any volume of 
weight. Here in the painting extra toughness and length given to 
Hanuman's tail seems to indicate that with its weight and length 
Hanuman has sought to balance the mountain's weight carried 
opposite to it while flying.  The jewellery - golden girdle, 
anklets, armbands and large earrings are an adding feature to the 
majestic personality of Hanuman. The contrast of green and orange 
colours is typical of Raghogarh painting from Central India.

metadata.title: Indo-Sassanian Coin
metadata.brief_description:
Indo-Sasanian
coinage was major type of coinage dating back to the Post Gupta period namely
to the time of Gurjaras, Pratiharas, Chalukyas, Palas, etc. extending from
Western India and in the Gangetic region covering a period of several centuries
in course of which a progressive degradation and stylisation of what was
originally seen as the Sasanian design. It was around the beginning of the 9th
century that the coin designs deprived from the Sasanians were adopted in the
Gangetic region.

metadata.title: A Sketch from Album No 80
metadata.museum_name: National Gallery of Modern Art, New Delhi
metadata.main_material: Pen and ink and pencil on postcard
metadata.brief_description:
Nandalal had a strong
affinity for sketching, his subjects being the surrounding environs, people in
their everyday life or anything that he found fascinating. In this sketch, he
has depicted a man carrying a heavy bulk of leafs on his shoulder, executed in
fluid, bold strokes of ink. The artist has carefully rendered the muscle
tension of the figure in fine lines, that brings out the corporeality in the form.
metadata.detailed_description: The National Gallery of Modern Art, New Delhi acquired the largest collection of 6744 artworks of Nandalal Bose from his family some of which were exhibited in the Centenary exhibition organised by the museum in the year 1983. metadata.title: Punchmarked Coin
metadata.brief_description: The term ‘Punch mark coins’ was first coined by James Prinsep in the year 1835 C.E. Vast majority of scholars believe that the earliest Punch Marked coins were issued by silver smith or bankers, but later on, the state tookover the responsibility for issuing coins and it its prerogative.
metadata.detailed_description: South Asia has been the cradle of human civilisation since time immemorial. The river basins on the North and North western parts of India have been occupied by humans since 5000 B.C.E. These people were engaged in trade and commerce with people from several other parts of the world including other civilisations. Trading was initially done by using Barter system but, with the advent of more complex economic transactions the coins became the medium of commercial exchange. According to archaeological evidences the earliest coins in India date back to 6th-5th Century B.C.E. These are the famous Punch marked coins mainly made of silver and copper. Mostly rectangular in shape, occasionally square or round with symbols. These symbols were impressed by separate punches at various points on the surface of the coin. They often overlap and on certain coins where the symbols are numerous, this overlapping results in a confused jumble. Rarely are obverse and reverse both the surfaces of the coin equally crowded with symbols. These symbols are regarded as the official guarantee of the validity of the coin and are considered, variously, as of religious or mythological in nature, or as official marks or symbols of the ruling dynasty or king, or as marks of bankers.

metadata.title: Image of Narasimha
metadata.brief_description:
Image of Narasimha assassinating the demon
Hirnyakashapu. The hybrid god has flowing hairs reaching up to shoulders and
secured by a crown. He wears hara, armlets and bracelets. The god is standing
on his right leg while his left foot is placed on a demon crawling beneath the
left leg. The body of the demon Hirnyakahapu is thrown on the thighs of left
leg and the god is engaged in tearing it with his two hands. His other two
hands are in abhaya-mudra and hold shankha. The demon holds a dagger and a
shield. The back slab behind shows a horizontal beam upon which lotus petals
are carved.

metadata.title: Album No 14 (A Sketch)
metadata.museum_name: National Gallery of Modern Art, New Delhi
metadata.brief_description: Nandalal, in his sketches, experimented with the representation of lines to attain texture and add vigour and life to the form of his figures. This sketch portrays a human figure in a seated position on the floor from the back, rendered in quick and subtle contouring lines. The fluid handling of the line and the dark ink strokes suggest the modelling of the body, sparing much detail.
metadata.detailed_description: Nandalal Bose had a strong affinity for sketching, his subjects being the surrounding environs, people in their everyday life or anything that he found fascinating. This album mostly consists of portraits and figurative sketches of people. Their myriad poses served as templates for his sketching routines. Nandalal's sketches vastly vary in detailing, some drawn quickly with just a few lines, others executed with more elaborate characteristics from an analytical perspective. In his book 'On Art', he writes, "An oriental artist need not start with painstaking labour of acquiring anatomical knowledge. For him an understanding of the life-movement shall suffice as a beginning. The principal life-movement in his drawing should never be lost or obscured in a temptation to lay stress on details of form and features."

metadata.title: Punchmarked Coin
metadata.brief_description: The term ‘Punch mark coins’ was first coined by James Prinsep in the year 1835 C.E. Vast majority of scholars believe that the earliest Punch Marked coins were issued by silver smith or bankers, but later on, the state tookover the responsibility for issuing coins and it its prerogative.
metadata.detailed_description: South Asia has been the cradle of human civilisation since time immemorial. The river basins on the North and North western parts of India have been occupied by humans since 5000 B.C.E. These people were engaged in trade and commerce with people from several other parts of the world including other civilisations. Trading was initially done by using Barter system but, with the advent of more complex economic transactions the coins became the medium of commercial exchange. According to archaeological evidences the earliest coins in India date back to 6th-5th Century B.C.E. These are the famous Punch marked coins mainly made of silver and copper. Mostly rectangular in shape, occasionally square or round with symbols. These symbols were impressed by separate punches at various points on the surface of the coin. They often overlap and on certain coins where the symbols are numerous, this overlapping results in a confused jumble. Rarely are obverse and reverse both the surfaces of the coin equally crowded with symbols. These symbols are regarded as the official guarantee of the validity of the coin and are considered, variously, as of religious or mythological in nature, or as official marks or symbols of the ruling dynasty or king, or as marks of bankers.

metadata.title: A Sketch from Album No 94
metadata.museum_name: National Gallery of Modern Art, New Delhi
metadata.brief_description:
In this pen and ink sketch,
Nandalal has depicted a vast rural landscape with a man sitting and playing
with a lattu in the foreground. He has portrayed the figure in quick, short
strokes to give a textured rendition to its body, resulting in a realistic
representation of the subject.
metadata.detailed_description: Nandalal Bose had a firm belief that for the creation of art an intimate understanding of nature and its ways is imperative. This particular album from the collection of the National Gallery of Modern Art, New Delhi showcases his search for newer forms of expression and his inner urge to constantly innovate with various subjects and media. Dinkar Kowshik in an article 'Drawings and Sketches of Nandalal' in the book, 'Nandalal Bose - A Collection of Essays' writes, "He (Nandalal) was indefatigable in his search for form and to the end of his life he remained a student. Whatever he saw, and wherever he went he recorded the flora and fauna, the people of the place, their dress, their carriages, the headdresses, the landscape, the festivals, the architecture; and while doing that he went on attaining a felicity of expression."

metadata.title: Motion and Stillness, Wadiaji, Atash Bahram, Bombay, 1983
metadata.museum_name: National Gallery of Modern Art, New Delhi
metadata.brief_description:
Sooni Taraporevala’s photographs are a
comprehensive presentation of the Parsi community depicting their traditions and way of life. In the above image, she has photographed a Parsi devotee praying in the Wadia Atash Bahram in Bombay. Atash Bahram is the highest grade of fire in the Zoroastrian temple. Taraporevala has captured the spirit of the Parsi community by
photographing its people sans
any choreography.
metadata.detailed_description: Sooni Taraporevala in her photography has documented the Parsi community, the followers of Zoroastrian religion and its dimensions, for the last three decades. What started as a personal memoir, soon shaped into a document for the world to acknowledge. The photographs are an absolute visual treat charged with human emotions of laughter, mirth, anguish, concern, all accentuating the warmth of relations of the subjects towards one another.

metadata.brief_description: The Earth Goddess carved on a gold leaf was found with a small deposit of human bones inside a funeral mound at Lauriya in the district of Champaran. The excavations were conducted by N.G.Majumdar in March,1905, and the identification of the female figure as Prithivi, the Earth Goddess, has been made with the help of a verse in the Rigveda.

metadata.title: Punchmarked coin
metadata.brief_description:
The
term ‘Punch mark coins’ was first coined by James Prinsep in the year 1835 C.E.
Vast majority of scholars believe that the earliest Punch Marked coins were
issued by silver smith or bankers, but later on, the state took over the
responsibility for issuing coins and it its prerogative.
metadata.detailed_description: South Asia has been the cradle of human civilisation since time immemorial. The river basins on the North and North western parts of India have been occupied by humans since 5000 B.C.E. These people were engaged in trade and commerce with people from several other parts of the world including other civilisations. Trading was initially done by using Barter system but, with the advent of more complex economic transactions the coins became the medium of commercial exchange. According to archaeological evidences the earliest coins in India date back to 6th-5th Century B.C.E. These are the famous Punch marked coins mainly made of silver and copper. Mostly rectangular in shape, occasionally square or round with symbols. These symbols were impressed by separate punches at various points on the surface of the coin. They often overlap and on certain coins where the symbols are numerous, this overlapping results in a confused jumble. Rarely are obverse and reverse both the surfaces of the coin equally crowded with symbols. These symbols are regarded as the official guarantee of the validity of the coin and are considered, variously, as of religious or mythological in nature, or as official marks or symbols of the ruling dynasty or king, or as marks of bankers.

metadata.title: Album No 14 (A Sketch)
metadata.museum_name: National Gallery of Modern Art, New Delhi
metadata.brief_description: Nandalal, in his sketches, experimented with the representation of lines to attain texture and add vigour and life to the form of his figures. This sketch portrays a human figure in a seated position on the floor from the back, rendered in quick and subtle contouring lines. The fluid handling of the line and the dark ink strokes suggest the modelling of the body, sparing much detail.
metadata.detailed_description: Nandalal Bose had a strong affinity for sketching, his subjects being the surrounding environs, people in their everyday life or anything that he found fascinating. This album mostly consists of portraits and figurative sketches of people. Their myriad poses served as templates for his sketching routines. Nandalal's sketches vastly vary in detailing, some drawn quickly with just a few lines, others executed with more elaborate characteristics from an analytical perspective. In his book 'On Art', he writes, "An oriental artist need not start with painstaking labour of acquiring anatomical knowledge. For him an understanding of the life-movement shall suffice as a beginning. The principal life-movement in his drawing should never be lost or obscured in a temptation to lay stress on details of form and features."

metadata.title: Image of Narasimha
metadata.brief_description:
Image of Narasimha assassinating the demon
Hirnyakashapu. The hybrid god has flowing hairs reaching up to shoulders and
secured by a crown. He wears hara, armlets and bracelets. The god is standing
on his right leg while his left foot is placed on a demon crawling beneath the
left leg. The body of the demon Hirnyakahapu is thrown on the thighs of left
leg and the god is engaged in tearing it with his two hands. His other two
hands are in abhaya-mudra and hold shankha. The demon holds a dagger and a
shield. The back slab behind shows a horizontal beam upon which lotus petals
are carved.

metadata.title: Indo-Sassanian Coin
metadata.brief_description:
Indo-Sasanian
coinage was major type of coinage dating back to the Post Gupta period namely
to the time of Gurjaras, Pratiharas, Chalukyas, Palas, etc. extending from
Western India and in the Gangetic region covering a period of several centuries
in course of which a progressive degradation and stylisation of what was
originally seen as the Sasanian design. It was around the beginning of the 9th
century that the coin designs deprived from the Sasanians were adopted in the
Gangetic region.

metadata.detailed_description: The sculpture is broken so that only the head and part of the right shoulder remain.The Buddha, nimbate, wears a diaphanous samghati with raised ridges depicting folds as are found on Buddha images of the Gupta period from Mathura. Folds of this type are not generally seen on images from this area. The face has plump cheeks, a bow-shaped mouth, and raised eye-brows. The folds of flesh on the neck are emphasized. A flying Deva bearing a garland can be seen above the figure. Part of the cross-bar of a throne back can be seen in low relief behind the right shoulder. Stylistically this head is reminiscentury of Sarnath images made after the fifth century CE.

metadata.title: Album No 14 (A Sketch)
metadata.museum_name: National Gallery of Modern Art, New Delhi
metadata.brief_description: Nandalal, in his sketches, experimented with the representation of lines to attain texture and add vigour and life to the form of his figures. This sketch portrays a human figure in a seated position on the floor from the back, rendered in quick and subtle contouring lines. The fluid handling of the line and the dark ink strokes suggest the modelling of the body, sparing much detail.
metadata.detailed_description: Nandalal Bose had a strong affinity for sketching, his subjects being the surrounding environs, people in their everyday life or anything that he found fascinating. This album mostly consists of portraits and figurative sketches of people. Their myriad poses served as templates for his sketching routines. Nandalal's sketches vastly vary in detailing, some drawn quickly with just a few lines, others executed with more elaborate characteristics from an analytical perspective. In his book 'On Art', he writes, "An oriental artist need not start with painstaking labour of acquiring anatomical knowledge. For him an understanding of the life-movement shall suffice as a beginning. The principal life-movement in his drawing should never be lost or obscured in a temptation to lay stress on details of form and features."

metadata.title: Pillar
metadata.brief_description:
Dwarfish square pillar, badly damaged with its two sides.  It has a 
square base decorated with highly ornamental Ghata-pallava motif 
and an octagonal shaft the faces of which are covered with 
floriated bands. The square abacus is decorated with Kirltimukhas 
emitting floriated scrolls. The capital rests on a triple molding. 
On the top there is an ardhapadma motif with over flowing foliage 
at the corners.

square base decorated with highly ornamental Ghata-pallava motif 
and an octagonal shaft the faces of which are covered with 
floriated bands. The square abacus is decorated with Kirltimukhas 
emitting floriated scrolls. The capital rests on a triple molding. 
On the top there is an ardhapadma motif with over flowing foliage 
at the corners.

metadata.title: A Sketch from Album No 94
metadata.museum_name: National Gallery of Modern Art, New Delhi
metadata.brief_description:
In this pen and ink sketch,
Nandalal has depicted a vast rural landscape with a man sitting and playing
with a lattu in the foreground. He has portrayed the figure in quick, short
strokes to give a textured rendition to its body, resulting in a realistic
representation of the subject.
metadata.detailed_description: Nandalal Bose had a firm belief that for the creation of art an intimate understanding of nature and its ways is imperative. This particular album from the collection of the National Gallery of Modern Art, New Delhi showcases his search for newer forms of expression and his inner urge to constantly innovate with various subjects and media. Dinkar Kowshik in an article 'Drawings and Sketches of Nandalal' in the book, 'Nandalal Bose - A Collection of Essays' writes, "He (Nandalal) was indefatigable in his search for form and to the end of his life he remained a student. Whatever he saw, and wherever he went he recorded the flora and fauna, the people of the place, their dress, their carriages, the headdresses, the landscape, the festivals, the architecture; and while doing that he went on attaining a felicity of expression."

metadata.title: Punchmarked Coin
metadata.brief_description: The term ‘Punch mark coins’ was first coined by James Prinsep in the year 1835 C.E. Vast majority of scholars believe that the earliest Punch Marked coins were issued by silver smith or bankers, but later on, the state tookover the responsibility for issuing coins and it its prerogative.
metadata.detailed_description: South Asia has been the cradle of human civilisation since time immemorial. The river basins on the North and North western parts of India have been occupied by humans since 5000 B.C.E. These people were engaged in trade and commerce with people from several other parts of the world including other civilisations. Trading was initially done by using Barter system but, with the advent of more complex economic transactions the coins became the medium of commercial exchange. According to archaeological evidences the earliest coins in India date back to 6th-5th Century B.C.E. These are the famous Punch marked coins mainly made of silver and copper. Mostly rectangular in shape, occasionally square or round with symbols. These symbols were impressed by separate punches at various points on the surface of the coin. They often overlap and on certain coins where the symbols are numerous, this overlapping results in a confused jumble. Rarely are obverse and reverse both the surfaces of the coin equally crowded with symbols. These symbols are regarded as the official guarantee of the validity of the coin and are considered, variously, as of religious or mythological in nature, or as official marks or symbols of the ruling dynasty or king, or as marks of bankers.

metadata.title: Royal Saint
metadata.brief_description:
A royal
saint having flowing moustache and well-trimmed bearded, standing on a
double-lotus pedestal, which is mounted on a square block with a flat base
having lotus petal decoration all around. The weight is taken on right leg. The
left leg having flexed and slightly advanced. Both the hands are joined against
the breast in Anjali-mudra. Other details include a fillet with tassels, two
necklaces, a beaded hara, patra-kundalas , armlets, bracelets etc.

metadata.title: A Sketch from Album No 94
metadata.museum_name: National Gallery of Modern Art, New Delhi
metadata.brief_description:
In this pen and ink sketch,
Nandalal has depicted a vast rural landscape with a man sitting and playing
with a lattu in the foreground. He has portrayed the figure in quick, short
strokes to give a textured rendition to its body, resulting in a realistic
representation of the subject.
metadata.detailed_description: Nandalal Bose had a firm belief that for the creation of art an intimate understanding of nature and its ways is imperative. This particular album from the collection of the National Gallery of Modern Art, New Delhi showcases his search for newer forms of expression and his inner urge to constantly innovate with various subjects and media. Dinkar Kowshik in an article 'Drawings and Sketches of Nandalal' in the book, 'Nandalal Bose - A Collection of Essays' writes, "He (Nandalal) was indefatigable in his search for form and to the end of his life he remained a student. Whatever he saw, and wherever he went he recorded the flora and fauna, the people of the place, their dress, their carriages, the headdresses, the landscape, the festivals, the architecture; and while doing that he went on attaining a felicity of expression."

metadata.title: Ma'bar Coin
metadata.brief_description:
Madurai Sultanate also known as Ma’bar
Sultanate was a short lived dynasty based in the city of Madurai in present day
Tamil Nadu. The Sultanate was declared independent by Jalal-ud-Din Ahsan Khan
from the Delhi Sultanate. The time span of Madurai Sultanate was very brief
lasting for a period of forty three years ruled by eight different rulers.

metadata.title: Punchmarked Coin
metadata.brief_description: The term ‘Punch mark coins’ was first coined by James Prinsep in the year 1835 C.E. Vast majority of scholars believe that the earliest Punch Marked coins were issued by silver smith or bankers, but later on, the state tookover the responsibility for issuing coins and it its prerogative.
metadata.detailed_description:
South Asia has been the cradle of human civilisation since time immemorial. The river basins on the North and North western parts on India have been occupied by humans since 5000 B.C.E. These people engaged in trade and commerce with people from several other parts of the world including other civilisations. Trading was initially done by using Barter system but, with the advent of more complex economic transactions the coins became the medium of commercial exchange. 
According to archaeological evidences the earliest coins in India date back to 6th-5th Century B.C.E. These are the famous Punch marked coins mainly made of silver and copper. Mostly rectangular in shape, occasionally square or round with symbols. These symbols were impressed by separate punches at various points on the surface of the coin.
They often overlap and on certain coins where the symbols are numerous, this overlapping results in a confused jumble. Rarely are obverse and reverse both the surfaces of the coin equally crowded with symbols. These symbols are regarded as the official guarantee of the validity of the coin and are considered, variously, as of religious or mythological in nature, or as official marks or symbols of the ruling dynasty or king, or as marks of bankers.

metadata.title: Royal Saint
metadata.brief_description:
A royal
saint having flowing moustache and well-trimmed bearded, standing on a
double-lotus pedestal, which is mounted on a square block with a flat base
having lotus petal decoration all around. The weight is taken on right leg. The
left leg having flexed and slightly advanced. Both the hands are joined against
the breast in Anjali-mudra. Other details include a fillet with tassels, two
necklaces, a beaded hara, patra-kundalas , armlets, bracelets etc.

metadata.brief_description: The Earth Goddess carved on a gold leaf was found with a small deposit of human bones inside a funeral mound at Lauriya in the district of Champaran. metadata.title: Ma'bar Coin
metadata.brief_description:
Madurai Sultanate also known as Ma’bar
Sultanate was a short lived dynasty based in the city of Madurai in present day
Tamil Nadu. The Sultanate was declared independent by Jalal-ud-Din Ahsan Khan
from the Delhi Sultanate. The time span of Madurai Sultanate was very brief
lasting for a period of forty three years ruled by eight different rulers.

metadata.title: Hanuman carrying Mount Dron with Sanjivini herb
metadata.brief_description:
Hanuman who abounds in immense knowledge, unlimited virtue, 
incomparable might, wind's pace and earth's firmness is not only 
the widest known god but in the entire Hindu pantheon also the 
most widely worshipped divinity. Hanuman, the emissary of Lord 
Rama, is known by many names such as Mahavira, Mahabali, 
Sankatmochan, Maruti, Ramaduta and Bajrangbali. Uttering his name, 
considered to have mystic powers of a mantra, dispels evil and 
commemorating it brings success in examinations, politics, 
business, sports, family-life and other worldly matters. He is 
believed to undo adverse influence of unfavorable stars. 
This highly powerful image of the 'Ramabhakta', Hanuman carrying 
Mount Dron is a beautiful depiction of the valorous act by the 
Monkey god. In this painting he is depicted carrying Mount Dron, 
one of the most wondrous acts that he accomplished for his lord 
Rama. Hit by Meghanatha's celestial weapon, Lakshmana, the younger 
brother of Rama, was wounded and had fallen unconscious. The herb 
he could be revived with was available only on Mount Dron in the 
far north. Hanuman went to fetch the herb but was unable to 
identify it. He hence uprooted the entire mountain and brought it 
to Rama's camp in Lanka. 
In most of the Hanuman paintings and sculptures his tail has been 
usually projected with prominence. In the legends associated with 
Hanuman, he is said to have performed most of his valorous deeds 
including the burning of Lanka with his tail. Hanuman could expand 
it to any length, make it tough and put into it any volume of 
weight. Here in the painting extra toughness and length given to 
Hanuman's tail seems to indicate that with its weight and length 
Hanuman has sought to balance the mountain's weight carried 
opposite to it while flying.  The jewellery - golden girdle, 
anklets, armbands and large earrings are an adding feature to the 
majestic personality of Hanuman. The contrast of green and orange 
colours is typical of Raghogarh painting from Central India.

metadata.title: Ma'bar Coin
metadata.brief_description:
Madurai Sultanate also known as Ma’bar
Sultanate was a short lived dynasty based in the city of Madurai in present day
Tamil Nadu. The Sultanate was declared independent by Jalal-ud-Din Ahsan Khan
from the Delhi Sultanate. The time span of Madurai Sultanate was very brief
lasting for a period of forty three years ruled by eight different rulers.

metadata.title: Ma'bar Coin
metadata.brief_description:
Madurai Sultanate also known as Ma’bar
Sultanate was a short lived dynasty based in the city of Madurai in present day
Tamil Nadu. The Sultanate was declared independent by Jalal-ud-Din Ahsan Khan
from the Delhi Sultanate. The time span of Madurai Sultanate was very brief
lasting for a period of forty three years ruled by eight different rulers.

questions.explanation: This Numismatics and Epigraphy belongs to the Late Medieval Period (1200 - 1526) based on its stylistic characteristics.
